title: Emotions and Policy Views
authors: Yann Algan; Eva Davoine; Thomas Renault; Stefanie Stantcheva

tax_mentions.context: Survey B describes a carbon tax with cash transfers, raising gasoline price by 40 cents/gal and rebating $600 per adult; asks support and analyzes treatment effects.
tax_mentions.notes: Support measured on Likert scale; used in climate policy views index.

tax_mentions.context: Question asks support for progressive carbon tax levied only on high-income people, raising gasoline price by 40 cents per gallon.
tax_mentions.notes: Item used in redistributive climate policy analyses.

excluded_policy_mentions.reason_it_is_not_a_carbon_or_fuel_tax: International climate treaty on emissions targets, not a domestic tax instrument.
excluded_policy_mentions.page_or_section: Appendix A.13.6.5 Harris vs Trump Policy Proposals

excluded_policy_mentions.exact_term_used_in_paper: tax incentives for renewable energy projects / subsidies for electric vehicles
excluded_policy_mentions.reason_it_is_not_a_carbon_or_fuel_tax: Spending or tax-expenditure subsidies supporting clean technologies, not fuel or carbon taxes.
Regulatory prohibition of certain vehicles, not a price-based fuel or carbon tax.

inclusion_reason: Survey B experimentally studies how emotions affect support for a carbon tax with cash transfers and a progressive carbon tax, providing direct evidence on carbon-tax acceptability.
